topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a cheerful greeting from 'StarlightSeeker.' I clicked it, bracing myself for some witty retort, maybe a playful jab. Instead, the profile picture loaded, and my breath hitched. It was a grainy photo of the old municipal library, the one near the riverfront. My stomach plummeted. I scrolled down, my fingers trembling slightly over the keyboard, confirming the address listed in the bio. It was my city. I reread the caption, the casual mention of a local coffee shop, and a hot flush crept up my neck. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in my small apartment. I walked to the window, staring out at the familiar skyline, suddenly feeling exposed, as if every casual interaction I’d had with StarlightSeeker had been a carefully constructed facade.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed, trying to shake off a lingering annoyance from a work meeting, when I saw the profile picture. It was a candid shot of a park bench bathed in afternoon sun. My jaw tightened involuntarily. I clicked over, and the city name displayed beneath their handle was undeniably mine. A strange, prickly feeling started in my chest, a tightening that made my shoulders hunch up toward my ears. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to rationalize it—a coincidence, surely. But then I noticed the obscure reference in their recent post, a local landmark only someone who lived here would immediately recognize. My hands began to grip the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. I needed to disconnect, to erase this jarring realization, but I found myself staring at the screen, unable to look away, the quiet hum of the city outside feeling suddenly loud and judgmental.

[story 3]

"No way," I muttered, leaning back in my chair, my expression hardening into something unreadable. The message from 'Echo_Wave' was simple: "Guess we share a zip code." The words landed with a dull thud, instantly draining the energy from my previous conversation. I felt a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a sensation I couldn't quite control. I started pacing the length of my living room, my movements jerky and agitated. I stopped abruptly by the bookshelf, running a hand over the spines of old novels, seeking some anchor in the familiar. It wasn't coincidence; it felt like a deliberate, pointed observation. I picked up my phone again, my thumb hovering over the reply button, the urge to lash out battling with a sudden, profound sense of being completely seen.
